era_context: The late 15th century saw the consolidation of early modern European nation-states, Ottoman expansion in the Eastern Mediterranean, Ming Dynasty stability in China, and the final stages of the Reconquista in Iberia. This was a period of dynastic succession, religious conflict, and the early Age of Exploration.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (beyond Portuguese coastal trading posts)

events.title: Ottoman-Crimean Conflict Escalates
events.description: Tensions between the Ottoman Empire and the Crimean Khanate escalated in 1475, leading to Ottoman military campaigns. The Crimean Khan, Mengli Giray, initially resisted Ottoman expansion but was eventually forced to accept Ottoman suzerainty. This conflict was part of the broader Ottoman expansion in the Black Sea region and would culminate in the Ottoman conquest of Crimea in 1476.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman-Crimean conflict in 1475 is attested in Ottoman sources; the exact sequence of events is less clear than the final conquest in 1476.

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War Continues
events.description: The Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) continued with Ottoman forces maintaining pressure on Venetian holdings in the Aegean and Adriatic. In 1475, Ottoman naval dominance was evident, and Venice was increasingly forced to negotiate. The war was economically draining for Venice and militarily advantageous for the Ottomans.
events.certainty_note: The war's continuation in 1475 is well-documented in both Ottoman and Venetian sources.

events.title: Ming Dynasty Stability Under Chenghua Emperor
events.description: The Chenghua Emperor (r. 1464–1487) continued to consolidate Ming Dynasty power in 1475. The dynasty was stable, and the emperor was focused on internal administration and maintaining the tributary system. This period saw continued economic development and cultural flourishing in China.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: General Ming Dynasty stability is well-documented; specific events for 1475 are not prominent in surviving records.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration of African Coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers continued systematic exploration of the West African coast under King John II. Portuguese traders were establishing trading posts and forts, acquiring gold, ivory, and enslaved people. This exploration was laying the groundwork for the later Portuguese maritime empire.

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and merchant accounts

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast is well-documented; specific events for 1475 are less detailed in surviving records.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: Castilian-Portuguese Conflict Over African Exploration
events.region: Kingdom of Castile / Kingdom of Portugal
events.description: Tensions between Castile and Portugal over control of African exploration and trade escalated in the 1470s. Both kingdoms were competing for access to African gold, ivory, and enslaved people. These tensions would eventually be resolved by the Treaty of Alcáçovas (1479), which recognized Portuguese dominance in African exploration and trade.
events.certainty_note: Castilian-Portuguese tensions over African exploration are attested in royal records; specific events for 1475 are less detailed.

disconfirming_evidence: The Ottoman-Crimean conflict is sometimes presented as a straightforward Ottoman military victory, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex negotiations and the Crimean Khan's initial resistance. The conquest was not immediate or total; the Khan retained significant autonomy as an Ottoman vassal. Portuguese exploration is sometimes portrayed as a coordinated 'Age of Exploration,' but it was actually a gradual, commercially-driven process with no single organizing principle. The conflict between Castile and Portugal over African exploration is sometimes overstated; both kingdoms had different strategic interests and were not in direct competition for the same territories.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1475 is moderate in Western European sources and rich in Ottoman sources. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are available but focus on court politics. African, American, and Asian sources beyond China are sparse or absent. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events in Europe and the Ottoman Empire; economic and social change in Africa and Asia are underrepresented. The Venetian-Ottoman War is well-documented in both Ottoman and Venetian sources, but the broader context of Ottoman expansion in the Black Sea is less well-known in Western historiography.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman-Crimean conflict escalated to Ottoman conquest in 1476
Continued Ottoman pressure led to Treaty of Constantinople in 1479
Castilian-Portuguese tensions resolved by Treaty of Alcáçovas in 1479
